perturbation.original_text: Pursuant to and in accordance with Section 5-1401 of the New York General Obligations Law, the Parties hereto agree that this Amendment in all respects, and any claim or cause of action based upon or arising out of this Amendment, or any dealing between the Parties relating to the subject matter of this Amendment or the transactions contemplated hereby or the Company/Repairer relationship being established, shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the State of New York, U.S.A. as applied to contracts to be performed wholly within the State of New York (Exclusive of Section 7-101 of the New York General Obligations Law which is inapplicable to this Amendment).
perturbation.changed_text: Pursuant to and in accordance with Section 5-1401 of the New York General Obligations Law, the Parties hereto agree that this Amendment in most respects, and any claim or cause of action loosely based upon or vaguely arising out of this Amendment, or any dealing between the Parties somewhat relating to the subject matter of this Amendment or the transactions contemplated hereby or the Company/Repairer relationship being established, may be governed by, and construed in general accordance with, the principles of law of the State of New York, U.S.A. as applied to contracts conceivably performable within the State of New York (Exclusive of Section 7-101 of the New York General Obligations Law which is inapplicable to this Amendment).
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides that the agreement "shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the State of New York". The modified text introduces ambiguity by using phrases like "in most respects", "loosely based upon", "vaguely arising out of", "somewhat relating to", "may be governed by", "in general accordance with", and "conceivably performable", significantly weakening the governing law provision. This makes it unclear which specific laws apply and under what circumstances.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Obligations Law § 5-1401 allows parties to a contract involving at least $250,000 to agree that New York law will govern their agreement, regardless of whether the agreement has a reasonable relation to New York. By introducing ambiguity and weakening the language (e.g., changing "shall be governed by" to "may be governed by"), the modified text undermines the enforceability and certainty that the parties intended New York law to apply, potentially nullifying the benefits conferred by § 5-1401. The changes make the application of New York law discretionary rather than mandatory, contrary to the statute's intent to provide a clear and enforceable choice of law.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-1401 Choice of law General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 14 § 5-1401. Choice of law. 1. The parties to any contract, agreement or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, in consideration of, or relating to any obligation arising out of a transaction covering in the aggregate not less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars, including a transaction otherwise covered by subsection (a) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code, may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties in whole or in part, whether or not such contract, agreement or undertaking bears a reasonable relation to this state. This section shall not apply to any contract, agreement or undertaking (a) for labor or personal services, (b) relating to any transaction for personal, family or household services, or (c) to the extent provided to the contrary in subsection (c) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code. 2. Nothing contained in this section shall be construed to limit or deny the enforcement of any provision respecting choice of law in any other contract, agreement or undertaking.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that New York law "shall govern" the agreement, as permitted by NY GOL § 5-1401. The modified text uses weaker, ambiguous language like "may be governed by" and "in general accordance with," undermining the original intent to have New York law definitively apply. The change from mandatory ("shall") to permissive ("may") directly contradicts the purpose of § 5-1401, which aims to allow parties to unequivocally choose New York law to govern their agreement, regardless of its connection to the state, as stated in the provided snippet which says parties "may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties". The introduction of vague terms further exacerbates this contradiction, rendering the choice of law uncertain and potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: This Amendment shall not be varied or modified except by a written document duly signed by duly authorized representatives of both Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: This Amendment may not be strictly varied or modified unless perhaps indicated in a written document supposedly signed by individuals vaguely representing both Parties.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause requires a written document duly signed by authorized representatives for any modification or variation of the agreement. The modified text introduces several ambiguities. "May not be strictly varied" is weaker than "shall not be varied." The phrase "unless perhaps indicated" creates uncertainty. Replacing "duly signed" with "supposedly signed" questions the validity of the signature. Finally, "individuals vaguely representing both Parties" casts doubt on the authority of the signatories. These ambiguities make it unclear whether a modification is valid, even if it's in writing and signed.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Obligations Law § 15-301 generally enforces "no oral modification" clauses in contracts. The modified text undermines this by creating ambiguity about what constitutes a valid written modification and who is authorized to sign it. While the statute doesn't explicitly mandate specific wording for written modifications, it implies a requirement for clear, unambiguous evidence of the parties' intent to modify the agreement. By using language like "may not be strictly varied" and "supposedly signed," the modification makes it more difficult to determine whether a purported written modification is enforceable under § 15-301, increasing the likelihood of disputes about contract terms.

General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 15, TITLE 3 § 15-301. When written agreement or other instrument cannot be changed by oral executory agreement, or discharged or terminated by oral executory agreement or oral consent or by oral notice. 1. A written agreement or other written instrument which contains a provision to the effect that it cannot be changed orally, cannot be changed by an executory agreement unless such executory agreement is in writing and signed by the party against whom enforcement of the change is sought or by his agent. 2. A written agreement or other written instrument which contains a provision to the effect that it cannot be terminated orally, cannot be discharged by an executory agreement unless such executory agreement is in writing and signed by the party against whom enforcement of the discharge is sought, or by his agent, and cannot be terminated by mutual consent unless such termination is effected by an executed accord and satisfaction other than the substitution of one executory contract for another, or is evidenced by a writing signed by the party against whom it is sought to enforce the termination, or by his agent. 3. a. A discharge or partial discharge of obligations under a written agreement or other written instrument is a change of the agreement or instrument for the purpose of subdivision one of this section and is not a discharge or termination for the purpose of subdivision two, unless all executory obligations under the agreement or instrument are discharged or terminated. b. A discharge or termination of all executory obligations under a written agreement or other written instrument is a discharge or termination for the purpose of subdivision two even though accrued obligations remaining unperformed at the date of the discharge or termination are not affected by it. c. perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the intent of New York General Obligations Law § 15-301, as the modified clause introduces ambiguity regarding the requirements for a valid written modification and authorized signatories, which undermines the purpose of ensuring clear and unambiguous evidence of the parties' intent to modify the agreement. The original clause requires a written document duly signed by authorized representatives for any modification or variation of the agreement. The modified text introduces several ambiguities. 'May not be strictly varied' is weaker than 'shall not be varied.' The phrase 'unless perhaps indicated' creates uncertainty. Replacing 'duly signed' with 'supposedly signed' questions the validity of the signature. Finally, 'individuals vaguely representing both Parties' casts doubt on the authority of the signatories. These ambiguities make it unclear whether a modification is valid, even if it's in writing and signed. As the LLM Law Explanation states 'By using language like "may not be strictly varied" and "supposedly signed," the modification makes it more difficult to determine whether a purported written modification is enforceable under § 15-301, increasing the likelihood of disputes about contract terms.' Scraped Law Snippet 2 references '§ 15-301. When written agreement or other instrument cannot be changed by oral executory agreement, or discharged or terminated by oral executory agreement or oral consent or by oral notice' which is the exact legal text that is being undermined.
